items.question: It's very likely: If I ___ time tonight, I'll call you.
explanations: Correct! A likely future takes the first conditional: 'If I have time'.
'Had' is the second conditional, for imaginary situations.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
'Would have' belongs to the third conditional.

items.question: It's just a dream: If I ___ a castle, I'd invite everyone.
explanations: 'Own' (present) is the first conditional, for likely situations.
Correct! An imaginary situation takes the second conditional: 'If I owned a castle'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
'Have owned' is present perfect, which doesn't fit here.

items.question: If it rains tomorrow, we ___ the match.
explanations: 'Cancel' (present) is for the if-clause, not the result.
Correct! A real future result uses 'will': 'we'll cancel the match'.
'Would cancel' is the second conditional, for imaginary situations.
'Cancelled' is past simple, used in the second conditional's if-clause.

items.question: If I ___ you, I'd take the job.
explanations: 'Am' is present simple, but this is imaginary advice.
Correct! Advice uses the second conditional: 'If I were you'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
'Had been' is the third conditional, about the past.

items.question: Which is a real, likely plan?
options: If I had a day off, I'd sleep all day.
If I have a day off next week, I'll visit my parents.
If I were free, I'd help you.
If I won, I'd share the money.
explanations: This is the second conditional — imaginary.
Correct! First conditional = a real, likely plan: 'If I have a day off, I'll visit my parents'.
This is the second conditional — imaginary.
This is the second conditional — imaginary/unlikely.

items.question: If she studies hard, she ___ the exam.
explanations: 'Passes' (present) is for the if-clause, not the result.
Correct! A likely result uses 'will': 'she'll pass the exam'.
'Would pass' is the second conditional, for imaginary situations.
'Passed' is past simple, used in the second conditional.

items.question: Correct this: 'If I had time now, I'll help you.'
options: If I had time now, I'd help you.
If I have time now, I'd help you.
If I had time now, I helped you.
explanations: Correct! 'Had' signals imaginary, so the result needs 'would': 'If I had time now, I'd help you'.
This mixes present + would; keep the tenses matched.
'I helped you' is past simple, not the imagined result.
It mixes a second-conditional if-clause with a first-conditional result.

items.question: If you ___ me tomorrow, I'll send the file.
explanations: Correct! A likely future takes the present simple: 'If you email me tomorrow'.
'Emailed' is the second conditional, for imaginary situations.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: It's very unlikely: If I ___ the president, I would change the law.
explanations: 'Am' is present simple, but this is an unlikely, imaginary situation.
Correct! An unlikely situation takes the second conditional with 'were': 'If I were the president'.
The if-clause never uses 'will'.
'Have been' is present perfect, which doesn't fit here.

items.question: Rewrite as imaginary: 'If I have money, I'll travel.' →
options: If I had money, I'd travel.
If I have money, I'd travel.
If I had money, I'll travel.
If I would have money, I'd travel.
explanations: Correct! The imaginary version is the second conditional: 'If I had money, I'd travel'.
This mixes present + would; both halves must match.
This mixes past + will; both halves must match.
The if-clause never uses 'would have'.
